structure.title: A key point to meet the challenges of developing automated driving functions, especially SAE J3016 automation level 3-5, is standardization: There is a huge potential in reducing development and validation costs by standardizing subsections of the complex hardware and software setup for automated driving functions. Sensors for detecting the vehicle environment and its connected environmental models are considered major enablers for automated driving. The current focus is on the standardization of sensor interfaces that serve as an input to environmental models or data fusion algorithms which in turn serve as the input for automated driving functions equal or greater than SAE level 3. Specifically, the focus is on interfaces for on-board sensors that can independently perceive their environment for performing safety critical tasks, e.g. camera, radar, lidar and ultrasonic sensors. This includes the actual measurement data as well as dynamically changing sensor performance values such as the currently estimated detection range. Other information sources like Car2X and maps are considered as potential extensions. With the resulting standardized sensor interfaces, OEMs, suppliers, service providers and tool providers can reduce their costs and time for the development and validation of automated driving functions.

nodes.title: 1.1 Intended audience
nodes.summary: The document explains the motivation, usage, and limitations of the Sensor interface standardization for autonomous driving on the AUTOSAR Adaptive platform. It emphasizes the interface's role as a critical connection between stakeholders in future vehicle architectures and software systems. The intended audience includes users of the AUTOSAR Adaptive platform and professionals involved in sensor-based system development for autonomous driving. The section further details distinct roles and their motivations for sensor standardization, organized by main technical aspects.

nodes.title: 1.1.3 System Integrator
nodes.summary: The System Integrator section emphasizes the necessity of a standardized interface and configuration description for enabling generic, testable software implementation and interoperability between systems. It highlights that such standardized descriptions are critical for Application Developers to achieve efficient fusion and algorithm implementation. The focus is on ensuring usability and consistency in interface definitions to support both integration and development processes. Technical details include the role of standardized descriptions in software interoperability and developer efficiency. The text underscores the dependency of AP Developers on these descriptions for effective system integration and algorithm design.

structure.title: 4 Scope of Sensor Interface Standardization
structure.summary: The sensor interface standardization builds on the ISO specification, focusing on semantic definitions, while the ADI specification for the AUTOSAR Adaptive platform includes syntactic elements and additional functionalities like sensor configuration to ensure full compliance. It excludes mechanical/electrical interfaces and raw data level interfaces, with raw data handling managed via the SWS Communication Management API. The main boundary condition is sensors connected via a service interface to the Adaptive computing unit, enabling direct connections to the ECU or data sharing between Adaptive ECUs. The scope emphasizes

structure.title: 5 Sensor Interface API Design
structure.summary: The chapter provides an overview of the rationale behind the current Interface API design proposal for Smart Sensors. It focuses on justifying the architectural choices and design principles guiding the API's development. The document emphasizes alignment with sensor functionality, interoperability, and scalability requirements. Technical details include considerations for data handling, communication protocols, and integration with existing systems. The rationale serves as a foundation for understanding the API's structure and intended use cases.

nodes.title: 5.1 Sensor Interface realization as a Service
nodes.summary: The Sensor Interface is realized as a Service to universally provide sensor functionality across AUTOSAR platforms (Classic and Adaptive). This approach supports future sensor architectures depicted in the accompanying figure. The figure, however, does not represent a valid scenario for Classic

nodes.title: 5.2 ISO mapping to Sensor Services
nodes.summary: The ISO standard defines Smart Sensors with object-level lists for tracking, roadmark, and landmark services, which are technology-agnostic. A separate sensor-technology-dependent feature list is used for detection. The Sensor Service design directly maps these ISO structures, ensuring each Smart Sensor provides tracking, roadmark, landmark services, and a detection service tailored to its technology. The figure illustrates this ISO-to-Sensor Service mapping.

nodes.title: 5.3 Sensor Service Template
nodes.summary: The Sensor Service Template addresses ISO's optional elements by distinguishing between mandatory and optional components, with optional elements predefined at design time by providers and users. A capability vector indicates which optional elements a sensor provides, while

nodes.title: 5.4 Interface stability
nodes.summary: The section highlights that the availability of affordable, suitable sensors is a critical driver for autonomous driving development. Extensive research and development in sensor technology continually introduce new capabilities, such as improved accuracy and higher sampling rates. These advancements complicate the creation of stable, long-lasting interfaces, as evolving sensor technologies make it difficult to define interfaces that remain consistent over time. The text emphasizes the inherent challenge of maintaining interface stability in a rapidly innovating field.

nodes.title: 5.5 Interface configuration
nodes.summary: The sensor interface configuration occurs at three distinct stages: **design time**, where all connections are pre-specified, compiled, and flashed, potentially requiring sensor updates; **connection time**, allowing multiple interface versions for a connection, with a handshake during system startup to select the optimal version and validate the configuration; and **runtime**, offering flexibility but complicating safety analysis due to unpredictable configuration variants. Runtime is deemed unsuitable for autonomous systems due to safety complexity and cost, while design time, though reliable, lacks flexibility. Connection time is proposed as a compromise, enabling adaptability without sacrificing safety, as demonstrated by use cases like adding

structure.title: 6 References
structure.summary: The "6 References" section lists a single reference: ISO-23150, which specifies the logical interface for data communication between sensors and data fusion units in road vehicles for automated driving functions. This standard defines the protocols and data exchange requirements for sensor integration in automated driving systems. It is cited as a foundational reference for the technical specifications discussed in the document. No other references are included in this section. The standard is relevant to the implementation of sensor-data fusion in vehicle automation.
